options.why: Took out $2$, a common factor but not the highest; $6x+9$ still shares a factor of $3$.
Took out $3$ instead of the HCF $6$; $4x+6$ still shares a factor of $2$.
Divided only the first term by $6$ and left $18$ unchanged inside the brackets.
Did not divide the second term $xy$ by the factor $x$.
Divided the second term by $x$ but left the first term as $6x$.
Multiplied the terms together instead of factorising the sum.
Took out only the numerical HCF $4$ and left the common factor $y$ inside.
Took out $2y$ instead of the HCF $4y$; $4x+6$ still shares a factor of $2$.
Did not cancel the $y$ when dividing the first term $8xy$ by $4y$.
Dropped the last term instead of writing the $1$ left after dividing $4$ by $4$.
Did not divide the last term $4$ by the HCF $4$.
Took out $2$ instead of the HCF $4$; the bracket is still all even.

questions.q: Which of these is the **fully** factorised form of $10a^2-15a$?

options.why: Took out only the numerical factor $5$; the bracket still shares a factor of $a$.
Took out only the factor $a$; the bracket still shares a factor of $5$.
Did not cancel the $a$ when dividing the second term $15a$ by $5a$.

questions.q: Amina wrote $4a-12b=2(2a-6b)$. What is the **fully** factorised form?

options.why: Left Amina's partly factorised form; $2a-6b$ still shares a factor of $2$.
Took out the full HCF $4$ but copied the old bracket without re-dividing the terms.
Divided $4a$ by $4$ but only divided $12b$ by $2$.
